versions.note: Near-perfect — and that's the warning sign, not the win
ROC-AUC 0.857 · sensitivity 93.6% · specificity 53.4% — the honest drop from 99.8%
Cross-lab specificity on unseen Barcelona normals 20% → 67% — multi-source training generalizes

epochSeries.label: v1 validation accuracy during training (climbs to 100% — itself the leakage warning)

changed: Tested the honest v2 model on two labs it had never seen (Aria/Tehran, Acevedo/Barcelona) — the real-world 'different microscope' test. It failed: on Barcelona's normal cells it flagged 80% as leukemic, and on raw Tehran images it scored worse than chance (ROC-AUC 0.40), exposing heavy dependence on C-NMC's black-background/stain style.
Retrained on TWO labs at once (C-NMC Delhi + Aria Tehran, real and black backgrounds), holding out Acevedo (Barcelona) entirely as an unseen third lab.
improved: Generalization to the unseen third lab more than tripled: specificity on Barcelona's normal cells rose from 20% to 67% (false-alarm 80%→33%; hard-negative lymphocytes 89%→36%).

Within-source performance improved too, not just held: 80.8%→83.3% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.857→0.863, specificity 53%→59% on the C-NMC held-out patients. Grad-CAM confirms attention on the cell cytoplasm, not the background.
knownGaps: 67% specificity still means a third of normal cells get flagged — improved, not solved. Aria is now a training source (its near-perfect v3 numbers are in-domain, not generalization — Barcelona is the honest proof), and a truly held-out external blast source would need a fourth dataset.

changed: Moved to the C-NMC 2019 dataset, which encodes a patient ID in every filename, and split the data by patient so no patient appears in both training and test (73 patients; 11 held out for the test set).
Reframed as a binary task — leukemic vs. normal cell — which is what C-NMC labels; a different, harder problem than v1's four-class subtyping, not a like-for-like rematch.
improved: Replaced v1's leakage-inflated 99.8% with an honestly-measured result on unseen patients: 80.8% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.857, and 93.6% sensitivity (it rarely misses a malignant cell).
knownGaps: Specificity is only 53.4% — the model over-calls malignant, because the training set has far more leukemic patients than normal ones. The test set is small too (11 patients, only 4 normal), so the per-patient view is noisy. Balancing the normal-patient data, or tuning the decision threshold, is the honest next step.

changed: First end-to-end build: a YOLO11s-cls network fine-tuned to sort blood-smear white cells into 4 classes — Benign plus three B-cell ALL maturation stages (Early Pre-B, Pre-B, Pro-B).
Reuses the MoleCheck computer-vision pipeline (transfer learning → ONNX for the browser, TFLite for the app).
Exported to ONNX (21 MB) running on-device in the browser demo.
improved: Learns the four cell types cleanly: 99.8% accuracy and 0.998 macro-F1 on the held-out images.
knownGaps: That 99.8% is a red flag, not a triumph. This dataset ships no patient IDs, so a random split almost certainly puts the same patient's cells in both training and test — the model can key on patient/staining artifacts instead of leukemia morphology. v2 (above) does the rigorous patient-level split, and the honest number drops to ~81%.
